recent_lessons: The Jalen Johnson DNP (confirmed OUT pre-game) was handled correctly by the voiding workflow for the individual pick, but his inclusion as a parlay leg in 'Soft Slate Scorers' created a structural parlay exposure that was not surfaced in the parlay rationale. The parlay hit because two legs covered and the parlay result was marked HIT despite a null leg — but the Parlay Agent should explicitly flag any leg where the player has a recent OUT history or a same-day injury designation, and require confirmation of availability before finalizing the parlay.

The session was concentrated in the 70–75% confidence band (8 of 11 picks), with zero picks in the 81%+ bands. While 100% hit rate at 70–75% is a strong session result, the calibration data now shows a structural gap: the system is not generating high-confidence picks (81%+) on most slates, likely because blowout caps and VOLATILE penalties are systematically pulling qualifying picks down into the 70–75% band. The Analyst should review whether the blowout spread cap thresholds (≥12 → 74% ceiling) are set too aggressively for primary scorers in matchups where the spread reflects opponent depletion rather than genuine garbage-time risk.

James Harden REB T4 missed (actual: 3 REB vs pick_value: 4) in a CLOSE game (CLE won by 5) — game script was not a blowout, meaning the standard blowout penalty applied during selection understated real variance risk. The 88% overall hit rate sounds strong, but in a close, contested game Harden's rebounding volume compresses as guard usage is focused on shot creation rather than board pursuit. REB props on guard-role facilitators (Harden averages well below center-level rebounding) deserve extra scrutiny even at low T4 thresholds — the 78% confidence was already at the exact floor, flagging marginal soundness.

Donovan Mitchell AST T4 missed (actual: 3 AST vs pick_value: 4) against tough ORL defense in a CLOSE game — the tier_walk correctly applied tough-defense and blowout penalties but still landed at 74%. In a CLOSE game where ORL's defense remained engaged all four quarters, Mitchell's creation sequences were disrupted below threshold. The 90% overall rate at T4 overstates true confidence when combined with tough defense and a co-creator (Harden) handling primary facilitation. When Mitchell and Harden share the floor, Mitchell's AST rate is more volatile — the without-Harden AST avg of 5.9 is not the operative baseline in games where Harden plays full minutes.

Kon Knueppel PTS T15 missed (actual: 14 PTS vs pick_value: 15) — the tier_walk correctly flagged SKIP under blowout_secondary_scorer rules at spread_abs=16.5, and the system's own reasoning classified this as a SKIP. The pick still appeared on the slate at 74% confidence (classified as SKIP in reasoning but included in the active pick count). The miss was by 1 point, consistent with the model's own pre-game concern: blowout minute compression for secondary scorers on the favored team. The SKIP classification in the tier_walk should have been a hard exclusion from active picks, not a soft trim. This is a model_gap_rule issue — the SKIP tag did not propagate to a voided pick status.

REB is the weakest prop type this session at 66.7% (2/3 hits) and both the Harden miss and the parlay miss trace to the same leg — guard REB props at T4 carry more intrinsic variance than the 88% historical rate suggests, particularly when the player is a secondary rebounder by role. The system's REB confidence floor of 78% is appropriately conservative, but the Harden pick was placed exactly at the floor after blowout penalty, leaving zero margin for game-script surprise.

Kevin Durant REB T4 missed at actual_value=3 (miss margin: -1) in HOU @ MIN, a 2-point game (CLOSE). The miss was not explained by game script — this was a close, full-minutes contest with no blowout compression. The root cause is the MIN rebounding environment: despite the MIN AST suppressor being documented, the system has no parallel MIN rebounding suppressor signal, and KD's 9/10 REB hit rate at T4 did not account for the possibility that MIN's frontcourt rebounding competition (active switching scheme, strong defensive rebounding) could suppress KD's counting stat even in a full-minutes game. The 9/10 rate provided false confidence at T4 for a road game vs. a strong defensive team without a specific REB DvP signal.

Scottie Barnes PTS T10 missed at actual_value=9 (miss margin: -1) in TOR @ LAC, a 25-point LAC blowout win. STEP 0: LAC won by 25, labeled BLOWOUT. Barnes was on the losing side. The pick's tier_walk did not apply a losing-side blowout penalty — the BLOWOUT_RISK field was not flagged (spread_abs=4.5 at pick time, suggesting the pre-game line did not anticipate a blowout). However, the system's blowout game-script logic for losing-side secondary scorers should have acknowledged that a TOR road underdog at LAC (spread_abs=4.5) carries meaningful blowout risk, and on the losing side even a consistent scorer can see Q4 minutes compressed. The 10/10 hit rate and 'consistent' tag created overconfidence at 84% for a pick that sat at exactly the T10 floor — a single reduced-minutes fourth quarter produced the miss. The top_pick flag on a road underdog with narrow threshold margin was too aggressive.

No misses this session — no analytical failures to log. However, three picks were human-flagged as trims: Banchero PTS/REB (blowout + minutes compression risk on a -15.5 spread), Banchero AST (VOLATILE tag compounding blowout script risk), LaMelo Ball PTS (minutes floor 21.8 below the 24-minute PTS safety threshold), and Miller 3PM (VOLATILE + exact minimum 7/10 threshold clearance). All four hit comfortably — Banchero played 38 minutes in what was ultimately a 4-point game, Ball scored 22 in 36 minutes, and Miller hit 4 threes. The SAC vs ORL final was 121-117 (CLOSE, not a blowout), which invalidated the -15.5 pre-game spread blowout concern retroactively. This is a calibration reminder: spread-based blowout penalties are pre-game risk adjustments, not deterministic outcomes — a tight final score on a large spread game is within normal variance and the underlying tier quality still drives results.

The proj_min=0 flag on Karl-Anthony Towns triggered a precautionary -5% confidence reduction in the tier walk, landing at 74%. Towns played 22 minutes and scored 13 points (HIT). The proj_min=0 field appears to be a data artifact or placeholder rather than a true projection — future runs should document this field's behavior more precisely and avoid applying ad-hoc confidence penalties when the player is NOT_LISTED on the injury report and full quant data is present. A formal rule clarifying when proj_min=0 should trigger a skip vs. a penalty vs. no action would prevent inconsistent handling.
recent_reinforcements: AST props on elite playmakers at conservative tiers (T2–T4) went 8/8 — the system's AST selection discipline, including correct application of the MIN suppressor exception at T2-level thresholds, is functioning at a high level. Ultra-low thresholds for high-usage playmakers (Harden T4, LeBron T4 iron_floor, Sengun T2, SGA T4) were the engine of the day.

The 70-75% confidence band went 11/11, confirming that the system's conservative floor-tier picks — where VOLATILE penalties, B2B step-downs, and suppressor discounts compress confidence — are well-calibrated. These lower-confidence picks are not marginal; they reflect sound methodology producing reliable outcomes.

The blowout-exemption logic for elite scorers (Luka PTS T25, SGA PTS T20) held up correctly: both hit comfortably despite game-script risk on the LAL side and a tough BOS defense. The FG_MARGIN_THIN step from T25 to T20 on SGA was conservative and correct given the 9% margin — SGA scored 33, validating that T20 was a sound floor.

Applying the Without-Star baseline for Julius Randle (Edwards OUT, n=14) as the primary baseline rather than overall season averages correctly elevated the PTS pick to T15 at 93%. The n≥10 threshold for treating Without-Star as primary was properly observed.

The 3PM card went 4/4 including two VOLATILE-penalized picks (KD, SGA) and one B2B step-down (Harden). The T1/T2 discipline for VOLATILE 3PM props — accepting the -5% confidence penalty rather than skipping — produced clean results on high-volume shooters.

Blowout-capped picks still delivered: all three Banchero props (PTS T10, REB T4, AST T4) were capped at 74% due to the -15.5 spread vs. depleted SAC, yet Banchero posted 30 PTS / 9 REB / 7 AST in 38 minutes — reinforcing that the blowout cap is a calibration tool, not a kill switch, and that elite primary scorers on favored teams against soft defenses will frequently clear even modest thresholds comfortably.

Iron-floor AST props at T2 were the most reliable structural play of the session: Desmond Bane AST T2 (90% hit rate, HIT 6), Brandon Miller AST T2 (100% hit rate, HIT 3), and LaMelo Ball AST T4 (95% hit rate, HIT 6) all cleared with room to spare. Low-threshold AST picks on primary ball-handlers and active secondary playmakers in competitive games remain a high-floor selection strategy.

The depleted-opponent stack logic proved correct: three CHA players (Ball PTS, Ball AST, Knueppel AST, Miller PTS, Miller AST, Miller 3PM) were selected against NYK missing key playmakers in a competitive H game (CHA won by 11). All six CHA picks hit, validating the approach of concentrating picks on the favored-by-usage side when opponent personnel is depleted.

Kon Knueppel AST T2 at 78% (9/10 hit rate, trend up) hit 8 assists — a 6-unit clearance over the T2 threshold. The upward trend tag on a secondary playmaker in a competitive home game with reduced opponent creation pressure is a reliable signal worth continuing to target.

Brandon Miller's 3PM iron-floor VOLATILE T2 pick cleared at 75% confidence (HIT 4), demonstrating that iron-floor status on a VOLATILE 3PM prop with a stable trend can still produce in a competitive game context — the iron floor provided the structural floor even with the VOLATILE penalty applied.
recent_recommendations: Institute a losing-side blowout buffer for road underdogs on PTS props near the threshold floor: when spread_abs is 4–7 (moderate underdog), apply a precautionary -5% confidence reduction to any PTS pick where pick_value is within 3 points of the T-floor (i.e., a near-threshold pick). A single short fourth quarter on the losing side produces exactly the -1 margin miss seen with Barnes today. This would have reduced confidence from 84% to ~79% or prompted a tier step-down on Barnes PTS.

Develop a MIN REB context flag analogous to the MIN AST suppressor. Today's KD REB miss (actual=3 vs. pick=4) in a competitive HOU @ MIN game is consistent with a pattern where MIN's frontcourt competition and scheme suppress opposing big-man rebounding. Track REB hit rates vs. MIN in the H15 backtest to evaluate whether a formal suppressor signal (similar to the AST suppressor at −29.5pp) is emerging for REB props as well.

For PTS top_pick designations on road underdogs, require at least one of: (a) spread_abs < 3 (near-even game), (b) iron_floor tag, or (c) pick_value at least 5 points below the player's L10 average. Barnes PTS at T10 was exactly at the tier floor with no iron_floor tag, on the road as a meaningful underdog — the top_pick flag elevated it inappropriately. Tighten this gate.

When a player's REB prop is targeted vs. a team with a documented defensive scheme signal (MIN switching, BOS switching, MIL switching), apply the same scrutiny logic used for AST props vs. those teams — prefer one tier lower or require a higher hit-rate threshold. The MIN AST suppressor framework is the right template; extend it to REB for known frontcourt-competition teams even before a formal backtest confirmation.

The 81-85% confidence band underperformed today (75.0% actual vs. 83.0% expected), driven by the Barnes PTS miss. Review all picks in this band for the pattern of 'consistent tag + 10/10 hit rate + near-threshold pick value on a road underdog' — this combination may be systematically overconfident. The 70-75% band's 11/11 performance suggests the system's conservatism at lower confidence levels is correctly calibrated; the 81-85% band may benefit from an additional gate requiring either iron_floor or pick_value ≥ 5 points below the tier threshold before emitting above 80%.

Formalize the proj_min=0 handling rule: when a player shows proj_min=0 in their quant header but is NOT_LISTED on the injury report and full hit-rate/tier data is populated, do NOT apply an ad-hoc confidence penalty. Instead, flag the field as a data artifact and proceed on injury report status alone. If proj_min=0 genuinely indicates non-participation, it should surface as an OUT in the injury report — that is the authoritative pipeline source. This session's KAT pick showed the penalty was unnecessary.

Maintain the AST iron-floor T2 strategy as a core selection pattern: Bane (9/10, T2), Miller (10/10, T2), and Ball (9/10, T4) all hit with substantial clearance. Low-threshold AST picks on primary and secondary playmakers in competitive games with soft-to-mid opponent AST defense are among the system's most reliable prop types. Continue prioritizing these when qualifying, especially in same-game stacks.

Revisit the Banchero blowout AST pick classification: the VOLATILE tag on a T4 AST prop in a -15.5 spread game correctly triggered a human skip recommendation, but the actual game finished as a 4-point CLOSE game (ORL 121, SAC 117). The pre-game spread did not materialize into a blowout. Going forward, when the VOLATILE tag and blowout cap combine to compress confidence below 72%, consider whether the pick should be skipped entirely at selection time rather than carried at reduced confidence — the risk-adjusted value of a 70% VOLATILE AST in a projected blowout is marginal, and the slot is better used on a cleaner prop.

Monitor the ORL vs. SAC game-script calibration: the pre-game spread was -15.5 favoring ORL, yet the final margin was only 4 points (SAC covered by a wide margin). This is a notable spread miss — the system applied blowout caps to three Banchero props based on a spread that did not reflect the actual game. Over time, tracking pre-game spread vs. actual margin calibration will help quantify how often large spreads vs. depleted opponents (SAC is 19-55, eliminated) produce genuine blowouts vs. competitive games, which should inform the blowout cap threshold for opponent-quality-adjusted spreads.

For the next CHA slate, continue targeting Brandon Miller AST T2 as a structural anchor: 10/10 iron-floor consistent tag, 3 assists again tonight (HIT), upward trend. This prop has the highest structural confidence on the CHA roster and should be a default consideration whenever Miller is active in a home game with mid-or-softer AST defense opposition.
